div.p: is no better than other men; when he dies he is but a bag of dust.1 I thank God for preserving me from my enemies;2 I have no enemies but for the truth’s sake.3 I have no desire but to do all men good.4 I feel to pray for all men.5 We don’t ask any people to throw away any good they have got; we only ask them to come and get more. What if all the world should embrace this Gospel? They would then see eye to eye,6 and the blessings of God would be poured out upon the people,7 which is the desire of my whole soul.8 Amen. (Jan. 22, 1843.) DHC 5:256–259.
To the Editor of The Wasp:—

Dear Sir: I have, of late, had repeated solicitations to have something to do in relation to the political farce about dividing the county; but as my feelings revolt at the idea of having anything to do with politics, I have declined in every instance in having anything to do on the subject. I think it would be well for politicians to regulate their own affairs. I wish to be let alone, that I may attend strictly to the spiritual welfare of the church.9
Please insert the above and oblige.
div.div: The Greatness and Mission of John the Baptist
div.p: The question arose from the saying of Jesus—“Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist; but he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.”10 How is it that John was considered one of the greatest prophets? His miracles could not have constituted his greatness.
First. He was entrusted with a divine mission of preparing the way before the face of the Lord.11 Whoever had such a trust committed to him before or since? No man.

Secondly. He was entrusted with the important mission, and it was required at his hands,12 to baptize the Son of Man. Whoever had the honor of doing that? Whoever had so great a privilege and glory? Whoever led the Son of God into the waters of baptism, and had the privilege of beholding the Holy Ghost descend in the form of a dove,13 or rather in the
